const item = $json;

const params = [
  item.content,                 // $1
  JSON.stringify(item.embedding), // $2
  item.title,                     // $3
  item.priority_level,            // $4
  item.energy_level,              // $5
  item.due_date,                  // $6
  JSON.stringify(item.metadata),  // $7
  item.notion_id,
  item.entity_type,
  item.scheduled,
  item.created_time,
  item.last_edited_time
];

return { params };

parameters.query:
INSERT INTO documents (
  content, embedding, title, priority_level, energy_level,
  due_date, metadata, notion_id, entity_type, scheduled, notion_created_time, notion_last_edited_time)
VALUES (
  $1, $2::vector, $3, $4, $5, $6, $7::jsonb, $8, $9, $10, $11, $12)
ON CONFLICT (notion_id)
DO UPDATE SET
  content = EXCLUDED.content,
  embedding = EXCLUDED.embedding,
  title = EXCLUDED.title,
  priority_level = EXCLUDED.priority_level,
  energy_level = EXCLUDED.energy_level,
  due_date = EXCLUDED.due_date,
  metadata = EXCLUDED.metadata,
  entity_type = EXCLUDED.entity_type,
  scheduled = EXCLUDED.scheduled,
  notion_created_time =
  EXCLUDED.notion_created_time,
  notion_last_edited_time = EXCLUDED.notion_last_edited_time;

parameters.content:
# What this workflow does
## Syncs a Notion productivity database to a PostgreSQL vector store. It polls Notion every night at 2 AM, detects updated pages, classifies each by type (Action, Project, Habit, Incubate, Inbox), builds a natural-language description of each item with its relationships, generates a semantic embedding via OpenAI, and upserts the result into a documents table with pgvector. This enables AI-powered semantic search over the user's personal task system.
